questions.question: What is interoceptive exposure and why is it considered the most beneficial component of CBT for panic disorder?

options.text: Avoiding all physical sensations to prevent panic attacks from occurring
Taking medication to block physical sensations during stressful situations
Deliberately creating feared physical sensations to teach your brain they are not dangerous
Gradually reducing exposure to stressful environments over time

questions.explanation: Interoceptive exposure involves deliberately creating the physical sensations you fear — such as a racing heart or dizziness — in a safe, controlled way. Research shows it is the most beneficial component of CBT for panic disorder because it provides direct, repeated evidence that these sensations are uncomfortable but not dangerous.

questions.question: When starting interoceptive exposure, what level of difficulty should you begin with?

options.text: The exercise that creates the most intense anxiety to face your biggest fear first
An exercise that creates moderate anxiety (4-6 out of 10)
Only exercises that produce no anxiety at all
Random exercises regardless of anxiety level

questions.explanation: The recommended approach is to start with exercises that create moderate anxiety (4-6 out of 10). Starting too high risks an overwhelming experience that could reinforce fear. Building confidence at moderate difficulty prepares you for harder exercises over time.

questions.question: What is the phenomenon called when fear naturally decreases through repeated exposure to a stimulus?

options.text: Desensitization
Habituation
Suppression
Avoidance conditioning

questions.explanation: Habituation is the process by which your brain learns that feared sensations are safe through repeated exposure. It is one of the most reliable phenomena in psychology — with consistent practice, the fear response weakens because your brain updates its threat assessment.

questions.question: What is the true goal of interoceptive exposure exercises?

options.text: To completely eliminate all uncomfortable physical sensations
To feel comfortable and relaxed during the exercises
To learn that the sensations are tolerable and not dangerous
To build physical endurance and fitness

questions.explanation: The goal of interoceptive exposure is NOT to stop the sensation or feel comfortable. It is to learn that the sensation is tolerable and not dangerous. You are building evidence against your catastrophic beliefs through direct experience — the most convincing evidence there is.

questions.question: Why should you write down your prediction before each interoceptive exposure exercise?

options.text: To have documentation for your medical records
Because writing is a relaxation technique that reduces anxiety
To create a clear test that you can compare against what actually happens
To identify which exercises you should skip

questions.explanation: Writing down your prediction (e.g., 'I'll faint' or 'I'll lose control') creates a clear, testable hypothesis. After the exercise, you compare your prediction to the actual outcome. The answer is almost always 'No, the catastrophe didn't happen' — and that repeated 'no' is the most powerful learning tool you have.
